The information below can be used to confirm the BundleID for a configuration policy.

Users report that �Per APP VPN� doesn�t work anymore on iOS devices because the certificates have expired. The certificates were initially deployed via a per app VPN profile. Users usually discover this when opening the app configured for �Per APP VPN� and recognizing that the app is failing to access data.
rootCause: This occurs because the certificates fail to automatically renew. In my case this was ICM 32775 (See https://icm.ad.msft.net/imp/v3/incidents/details/32775183/home).
solution: The workaround is to create a new SCEP profile and accompanying VPN/Wifi profile.  Once compliance reaches expected levels, remove the older SCEP profile and associated VPN and Wifi profiles. See 117091816352445 for more details.Related cases include:117040715572384117022294104190116122115093167

symptom:
�        
The �Windows Defender Security Center� is
showing unexpected results on Windows 10 1703/1709

In 1709, it will show everything as disabled (see below screenshot as an
example) despite settings being present.

o  


�        
You can confirm that Windows Defender Settings
deployed via Microosft Intune are present on the device, despite this interface
not showing up correctly. 

o  
Run PowerShell as an Administrator

o  
Run the command: �Get-MpPreference� and compare
the settings displayed via what is shown from thecommand output. 

o  
Confirm
that the variable: UILockdown is set to (True) 

o  


 

If the value of UILockdown
is set to �True�, and the
customer has the setting �Enable user
access to Windows Defender� is set to Allowed,
then this article shows a workaround for this
rootCause:
The �Enable user access to Windows Defender� is not working
correctly. Setting it to �Allow� actually prevents
the access to Windows Defender, by FORCING the UILockdown to be enabled.

The �Enable user access to Windows Defender� setting
modifies a CSP (Policy CSP > Defender > AllowUserUIAccess) and incorrectly
sets it.
solution:
The workaround, until a fix is implemented to resolve the
CSP being set incorrectly, can be found here.


 On
     your existing Windows 10 Configuration Policy that you�re deploying, change
     the �Windows Defender Antivirus > End-user access to Defender� to �Not
     Configured.� Save the current policy. 
 
  This alone will not fix the issue � setting it to �Not
      Configured� means the existing setting is simply not changed� eg; if the
      UILockdown is already enabled, it will stay enabled. However � it ensures
      new PC�s do not receive the setting to block the UI for Defender. 
 
 Create
     a new, custom setting to correctly enable User Access. 
 
  The setting we are overriding, is the Windows CSP
      (Configuration Service Point) that Intune sets via the UI (The UI sets it
      incorrectly, so manually setting it bypasses that)
  This setting is found here: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/client-management/mdm/policy-csp-defender#defender-allowuseruiaccess
      
  In Microsoft Intune, go to� 
  
   Device Configuration 
   Profiles
   Create profile
   Name: AllowUserUIAccess (you can name it what you want)
   Platform: Windows 10 and later
   Profile type: Custom
   Settings > Configure
   �Add�
   Name: AllowUIUserAccess
   OMA-URI: ./Vendor/MSFT/Policy/Config/Defender/AllowUserUIAccess
   Data type: Integer
   Value: 1
   Create & deploy the profile to the groups you
       specify. 
   
  
 


 

Once this custom setting is defined, deploy it to the
security groups of your choice.

Verification the policy worked: 

�        
View the Windows Defender Security Center� if
everything shows up fine and as expected, you know it works. 

�        
Alternatively, run the �Get-MpPreference� command, and view the variable of the �UILockdown�


o  
You can also run: Get-MpPreference | FL UILockdown and verify the variable is False. 

o

You can easily locate an Collection ID and location \ path of a collection in a Intune Hybrid SCCM Console that is lost or there are too many nested collections to walk through with the following process:1. Open SQL Studio2. Expand DB3. Expand CM DB4. Select Table - right click and select Filter settings5. Filter for collection and click OK6. Seelct Collections_G, right click and show top 10007. Find the CollectionName in the table, then find the Collection ID will be sitecodexxxxxx (Note: The returned data is a little mis-leading the CollectionID column is not actually the true CollectionID you want its the SiteID column examples below)8. Open a new query9. Run query: select FolderPath, InstanceKey from Folders a inner join FolderMembers b on a.ContainerNodeID = b.ContainerNodeID where InstanceKey = 'COLLECTIONIDHERE' 10. execute[Example]Here you see a example of a nested collection, while in this example its simple to find imaging a customer with hundreds of nested collections.Using the steps above we see the following results:Taking this collection ID, we perform the query to see the following results: As you can see you get the path of the collection so you can easily locate and navigate inside the console to the collection.

What inventory is collected per device. Mobile Device inventory collection properties including Name, Operating System, Manufacturer, Model, Management Channel, AAD Registered, Compliant, EAS Activated, Serial number, IMEI, etc. collected by Intune for all devices, iOS, Android, Windows, Mac OS X.

Microsoft/Pradeo Support Collaboration and Escalation Process for Intune integration issues. Template for opening support cases between Microsoft and Pradeo, including severity definitions and escalation contacts.

A PC or Windows tablet cannot be MDM joined. Error during MDM enroll: Your device is already being managed by an organization. Deployment of the full Intune client software failed. Error code 0x8004300F Stage 20. SCCM client prevents Intune enrollment.
rootCause: The machine cannot be MDM joined (prior to 1709), or the Intune PC agent software cannot be installed because the System Center Configuration Manager client is already installed, or a trace of a previous SCCM installation prevents enrollment.
solution: Remove the Configuration Manager client: run CCMSETUP.exe /Uninstall from elevated command prompt at C:WindowsCCMSETUP. If issue persists, use SMS 2003 Toolkit 2 CCMclean.exe with /all switch to purge all SCCM traces. Reboot and retry Intune enrollment.

symptom: How to capture a bug report from an Android device for diagnostic purposes. Bug report contains device logs, stack traces, and diagnostic information. Use Take bug report developer option, Android Emulator menu, or adb bugreport command.

When setting up the Intune on-premise Exchange connector, error: The remote server returned an error: (400) Bad Request. Error code 0x000000b. ConnectorRegistrar.ConfigureConnector2 throws WebException.
rootCause: Three possible causes: 1) Account used to setup connector is missing Intune license or is not global administrator. 2) Intune on-premise Exchange connector cannot communicate with Intune service over Internet. 3) Web proxy configured for SSL packet inspection causing 400 Bad Request during HTTPS communication.
solution: 1) Ensure account is global administrator and has Intune license. 2) Verify connectivity to all Intune endpoints from the connector machine. 3) Ensure SSL packet inspection is not enabled on proxy or networking appliances in the network path.

symptom: When setting up the Intune Exchange service-to-service connector, error message: An error occurred request ID: 6f140b49-b2bd-4e29-b335-5b8c053c354d.
rootCause: Account used to setup the connector does not have either Intune license or global administrator role.
solution: Assign the account used to setup the Intune connector an Intune license or assign it global admin role.

symptom: Adding second email account to Outlook app fails with Misconfiguration Alert: Your admin wants the apps on this device to be managed with the account. The app account you are using will be removed.
rootCause: By design. Enrolled and managed devices can only have one corporate email in Outlook.
solution: Intune supports deployment of app protection policies (MAM policy) to one user account per device. Documented at https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/end-user-mam-apps-ios.

symptom: iOS enrollment fails with: Profile Installation Failed. Connection to the server could not be established.
rootCause: Device was previously enrolled by a different user and the previous user device information is still in Azure. Can verify in Kusto with DeviceLifecycle query showing EnrollmentAddDeviceFailedEvent with Re-enrollment with different user.
solution: Login to Azure portal, go to Devices > All Devices, find the device using previous or new user UPN or serial number. Remove the device. Once removed, enrollment should succeed.

symptom: When accessing Microsoft Planner, error: "You can't get there from here. It looks like you're trying to open this resource with an app that hasn't been approved by your IT department."
rootCause: An Exchange Online conditional access policy was created using the classic Silverlight portal or the Intune App Protection (MAM) blade with Enable app access set to Allow apps that support Intune app policies.
solution: Remove existing conditional access policies in the Intune App Protection (MAM) blade and configure them in the Azure CA blade: 1) Disable existing Exchange Online CA policy. 2) Go to Azure AD > Conditional access. 3) Create new policy with same assignments, under Grant select Require approved client app.

symptom: In Azure Intune portal Device Compliance, devices with compliance policies assigned appear in report for devices with no compliance policy. Devices show Error status despite being compliant.
rootCause: Compliance policies are applied to devices instead of users or groups.
solution: Resolved with Intune 1803 service release.

symptom: After deploying a LOB application to MacOS or iOS, clients receive error: "Unable to Download App - <appName> could not be downloaded at this time".
rootCause: Invalid URLs in the .plist file for the app.
solution: Remove the invalid URLs or change them to valid URL addresses in the .plist file.

symptom: After deploying a LOB application to MacOS or iOS, error: "Unable to Download App" or "app package for iOS (*.ipa file) could not be downloaded at this time". Error code 0x87d1041C in Company Portal.
rootCause: The iOS Distribution Certificate for the app has expired.
solution: Obtain a new iOS Distribution Certificate from Apple, distribute a new version of the app signed with the new certificate.

symptom: Even when enabling Locked Enrollment in an Apple iOS enrollment profile, MDM enrollment completes but the user still sees the option to "Leave Remote Management" by removing or deleting the management profile. This is provisional DEP behavior.

Logs at C:ProgramDataMicrosoftIntune Management ExtensionLogs (AgentExecutor, ClientHealth, IntuneManagementExtension). Kusto queries for DeviceManagementProvider and IntuneEvent tables.

After creating and applying an Update Ring to Windows clients, the update does not start as expected. Guide for collecting diagnostic data to determine if client receives the policy from Intune.

Guide on restoring iCloud backups to ADE (DEP) iOS/iPadOS devices. Covers 7 scenarios: non-supervised/non-MDM/ADE backups restored to same/different ADE devices. Key: supervision state restores from backup on same device, from ABM/ASM on different device. Workarounds: restore to different device or skip iCloud restore.
Guide for testing LOB MSI deployments in Intune using 7Zip to verify MSI deployment functionality before troubleshooting customer-specific apps.

Customer has MAM policies applied to all Office mobile apps including OneDrive. Users already have the Office mobile apps installed on their personal devices, however they're signed in with their personal accounts. When the users then enroll in a BYOD scenario, MAM policy comes down and the users gets the following notification message as expected:Your organization is now protecting its data in this app. You need to restart the app to continue.The user then logs back in using their personal account (not the corporate account), browses to their corporate OneDrive and tries to open a corporate doc. That fails with the following error:Org Data RemovalYour organization has removed its data associated with this app. (607)To continue you must restart this app.To reconnect to your organization, sign-in to your work or school account.
rootCause: This can occur is the user is signed in to the Office mobile apps (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) with their personal account. This is by design.
solution: To avoid this error, once users enroll their device in Intune they need to login to the Office mobile apps using their company account.

symptom: When troubleshooting the deployment of IPAs to iOS devices like iPhones and iPads, before you attempt to troubleshoot the customer's particular app it may be a good idea to test and verify that Line-of-business IPA deployments work at all for any app. The recipe below shows how to configure a deployment for Simple Notepad to test and verify that IPA deployments work in your tenant or in a customer's tenant.
When viewing the status of Device Actions in the Azure Intune portal, some actions show a UPN in the "Initiated By" column while others just show a UserID GUID. For example:
rootCause: This can occur if the Azure user account used to initiated the device action doesn�t have an Intune license and therefore does not actually exist in Intune. For example, you can create a user in Azure AD (or Office365) and make them a global admin in your tenant, and at that point they can login to the Intune portal and initiate device actions even though they don�t exist in Intune. The device action will complete, but since Intune itself isn�t aware of that user, all it can display is the GUID, not the UPN.
solution: Once this is done you can�t go back and fix it, because even if you add a license to that user you�ll still only see the GUID in the report (it�s not a dynamic field). However, you can make sure it doesn�t happen going forward. Just make sure that any user performing device management in Intune has a valid Intune license.The screen shot below shows what you'll see once a license is added to the user and the user subsequently initiates another device action (remote wipe, retire, remove company data, etc).

symptom: When viewing the Audit Logs under Devices Actions in the Azure Intune portal, the INITIATED BY (ACTOR) column is blank and doesn't tell you who did a remote wipe, etc. For example:
rootCause: This can occur if the Azure user account used to initiated the device action doesn�t have an Intune license and therefore does not actually exist in Intune. For example, you can create a user in Azure AD (or Office365) and make them a global admin in your tenant, and at that point they can login to the Intune portal and initiate device actions even though they don�t exist in Intune. The device action will complete, but since Intune itself isn�t aware of that user it cannot display it in the audit log.
solution: Once this is done you can�t go back and fix it, because even if you add a license to that user you�ll still only see the GUID in the report (it�s not a dynamic field). However, you can prevent it from occurring in the future. Just make sure that any user performing device management in Intune has a valid Intune license.The screen shot below shows what you'll see once a license is added to the user and the user subsequently initiates another device action (remote wipe, retire, remove company data, etc).

symptom: After configuring and assigning an Update Ring policy to a group of Windows 10 Enterprise computers, the policy gets delivered correctly by Intune but the update never starts.
rootCause: This can occur if Automatic Updates have been disabled on the client. This will typically occur if the device is being managed by SCCM or the customer is using WSUS. Update Ring policy only comes into effect after the Windows PC checks for and finds the associated update available. If AU is broken and/or the Windows client never checks for updates, the update will never be installed.
solution: To verify when the Windows PC last checked for updates, go to System Settings -> Update & Security -> Windows Update. Under Update Status you should see when the computer last checked for updates. By default, Windows should be checking for updates at least once a day.Also, look in the Registry under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsUpdate\AU and check the following values:AUOptionsUseWUServerNoAutoUpdateIf these are set to a value of 1 then Automatic Updates may be disabled. For example, "NoAutoUpdate"=dword:00000001 disables automatic updates and �AUOptions"=dword:00000001 tells Windows not to check for updates. To fix Automatic Updates, configure those keys as follows and see if that fixes the problem.[HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsUpdate\AU]"AUOptions"=dword:00000004"IncludeRecommendedUpdates"=dword:00000001"UseWUServer"=dword:00000000"NoAutoUpdate"=dword:00000000

symptom: After configuring and assigning a managed iOS app from the App Store, the installation fails on assigned devices and error 0x87D12710 is seen in the status:
rootCause: This can occur if the URL for the app is malformed or incorrect. For example, there might be an extra space in the URL like this:https://myAppV2.contoso.com:8443 /
solution: To resolve this problem, correct the URL in the app package.

symptom: Remote Tasks performed by Helpdesk Operators in Intune fail with the error:Initiating Factory reset failed
rootCause: The scope of permissions for the Helpdesk operator role is applied to Dynamic Device Group. The Helpdesk Operator Role should be applied to User Groups
solution: The scope of permissions for RBAC permissions and the Helpdesk Operator Role should be applied to User Groups.

symptom: A user attempts to enroll an iOS based device via the Company Portal application and after selecting to install the management profile, the enrollment doesn't proceed and results with a file named "undefined" being displayed in the browser on the site, login.microsoftonline.com.
rootCause: The Safari browser is configured to "Block All Cookies".
solution: After configuring the Safari browser to allow Cookies, as below, attempt to enroll again. -Go to �Settings� -Select �Safari� -Under Privacy and Security turn off �Block All Cookies�

symptom: After configuring co-existence with Microsoft Intune and Office 365, when navigating to the Device Management tab in the O365 portal the following message is displayed:"You don't need to set up Mobile Device Management for Office 365 because your organization is already protected by Microsoft Intune".
rootCause: This can occur if your Mobile Device Management Authority is set to Configuration Manager.
solution: The only option to work around this issue is to set your MDM authority back to standalone Intune.

symptom: When attempting to access offline files in OneDrive when using an iOS device enrolled in Intune, OneDrive fails with a cavalcade of misleading errors. This happens even when no Intune policies are applied to the user or device. Repro steps are below:1. Create a user in O365 and give that user an Intune license. 2. Make sure there are no Intune policies or profiles of any kind assigned to the user. - Make sure conditional access is not enabled. - Make sure no app protection policies or apps are assigned to the user. - Basically we need NOTHING in Intune assigned to this user.3. Have that user get an iOS device and install the OneDrive app from the App Store.4. Configure OneDrive for both a personal account and the Office365 account.5. Open the OneDrive app and locate any file using the O365 account, them make that file available offline.6. Terminate the OneDrive app.7. Install the Intune Company Portal app from the app store and have the user enroll the device in Intune.8. Verify that the user can still access the offline file in OneDrive.9. Terminate the OneDrive app.10. Put the device into Airplane Mode.11. Open the OneDrive app. You will receive a message stating the following. This is normal:"Turn off Airplane Mode or Use Wi-Fi to Access Data"12. Click OK in the message, then you'll receive the following error:"Offline Grace Period ExpiredConnect to the Internet to continue access your work or school account in this app.Close Application"13. Click "Close Application". The app does not actually close but you then get the following error:"Access your filesYour IT administrator has required you to authenticate in order to access your organization's files.Enter"17. Click "Enter" and note how you get a blank screen (white screen). Your only option is to terminate OneDrive at this point.Expected behavior is that the user can access his offline files.
rootCause: This is caused by a bug in OneDrive.
solution: This is scheduled to be fixed in the January 2018 update for the iOS OneDrive mobile app.

symptom: If you deploy a PowerShell Script via the Intune Management Extension (see article here: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/intune-management-extension ) it does not work on Microsoft Surface Hub devices.
solution: This is considered by-design.As of 1/3/2018, Windows MDM does not support the execution of PowerShell scripts natively.To work around this, the Intune service deploys the Intune Management Agent application. This is a global win32 application that runs as a .exe application. This application calls the PowerShell executable, and runs the scripts deployed by the administrator. Please see here: https://internal.support.services.microsoft.com/en-us/help/4057391 Surface Hubs, however, cannot run these standard executable/win32 applications.Surface Hubs can only run Universal Windows Platform (UWP) applications (commonly known as Store Apps, etc).As a result, our agent cannot run on these Surface Hub devices at this time.

symptom: Annett Roosa (aroosa) and Patrick Lewis (patlewis) have been working on an Intune Management Extension troubleshooter and OneNote that details most of the issues found to date. Details can be found below.

Overview MDM Coexistence - using both Microsoft Intune and Office 365 concurrently on the same tenant to meet your mobile device management requirements. Introduction Customers looking to use a combination of the basic mobile device management (MDM) capabilities of the built-in (Mobile Device Management for Office 365) service and the more advanced MDM capabilities of Microsoft Intune (Intune) service can do so by participating in MDM Coexistence . Setting Up Mobile Device Management Coexistence If you are currently using Intune In a Browser Navigate to https://admin.microsoft.com/EAdmin/Device/IntuneInventory.aspx or https://portal.office.com/EAdmin/Device/IntuneInventory.aspx On the Set up Mobile Device Management for Office 365 click on the arrow next to Lets get startedIt can take a few hours to complete the process of enabling Coexistence.Once Process is complete you will be able to view your devices at: https://admin.microsoft.com/adminportal/home#/MifoDevices and manage the devices. If you are currently using Mobile Device Management for Office 365 1. Login to https://portal.azure.com as a global administrator.2. On the Intune blade, go to Overview.3. You will see a blue banner in the top middle pane: 4. Click on the banner. If the banner is missing, navigate to Quick Start, then return to Overview.5. In the Add MDM Authority wizard, check Intune MDM Authority and click Add. Who is Managed by What? Now that you can use both Mobile Device Management for Office 365 and Intune concurrently on the same tenant, the very first question that comes to mind is how to determine and specify who is managed by what? This is determined and designated according to the following rules: If a user has an Intune license assigned to her, either through Office (Office Portal) or the Azure (Enterprise Mobility Suite) Portal, then the user is managed by Intune If a user does NOT have an Intune license, but DOES has an Office 365 license assigned to her (through the Office 365 Portal), then the user is managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365 In other words, the Intune license supersedes the Office 365 license when it comes to determining which service is used to manage which user Switching Users Management Authority Another question that is frequently asked at this point is, how can we change a particular user from being managed by one service to another, and what is the effect and impact of such switch? If a user is currently being managed by Intune, simply remove the Intune license from the user, and make sure that the user has an Office 365 license assigned. At this point, the user is now managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365 If a user is currently being managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365, simply assign an Intune license to the user. At this point, the user is now managed by Intune As to what happens to a users previously enrolled device after the users management authority has been changed, there are two possible options and outcomes: 1. One approach is to make sure that the users existing enrolled devices are un-enrolled (either by the user or by the Administrator) prior to the switch, and then re-enrolled after the switch. 1. Advantage: by doing this we can ensure a clean break-up and switch. That is, any/all settings and policies previously applied to the devices will be removed, and upon re-enrolling the settings and policies from the new authority will be applied. 2. Disadvantage: it could often be very costly and time-consuming for organizations to un-enroll and re-enroll devices. 2. Another approach is to simply make the switch, and wait several minutes. After the switch has been completed, the devices will remain enrolled, and will be managed by the new authority moving forward. The settings from the new authority will be applied and overwrite the previous settings the next time the device synchronizes with the service. 1. Advantage: by doing this, there is no need to un-enroll or re-enroll the devices, and the devices will continue to remain protected. The transition can be seamless. 2. Disadvantage: because there was no specific un-enroll and corresponding clean-up action, and the new settings from the new authority will only overwrite the existing settings, there is the possibility of confusion (from residual previous settings) as well as potential gap in protection if the settings from the new management authority does not match or exceed that of the old authority. Displaying Who is Managed by What? When a tenant (and its users and devices) are managed by multiple MDM services, it is also important for the Administrator to be able to accurately and effective see who is managed by what take actions accordingly. Currently there is an outstanding issue that we are still working to resolve, so we need to make sure that all customers who are using coexistence are aware of this. More specifically, the issue is as follows: Today, when a tenant is using both Intune and Mobile Device Management for Office 365 concurrently In the Intune Azure portal, ALL users and devices managed by MDM will be displayed. In the O365 Portal, ALL users and devices , regardless of whether they are managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365 , will be displayed This could cause confusions to the Administrator when he is trying to manage devices in the O365 Portal, because when he selects a device that is managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365 , he will be able to perform management actions (such as Full Wipe or Selective Wipe), whereas if he selects a device that is managed by Intune , he will not be able to perform any management actions. Instead, he will simply see an error message stating that the Wipe actions are not available . (see screenshots on the following page for comparisons for these two cases) For organizations with large numbers of users and devices, this inability to filter out other devices and only display devices that are managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365 or Intune can be a significant management problem and challenge. We are currently diligently working on a solution to resolve this problem and will keep the participants in the beta program informed of the latest status of this solution. Setting APNs Certificate for managed iOS devices For tenants that have been on-boarded for MDM coexistence , another area to be aware of are settings that have tenant-wide impacts such as APNs certificate for iOS devices. Although APNs certificate can be set from either Intune Admin Console or O365 Portal (see screenshots below), once it has been set, the SAME certificate is used for BOTH Mobile Device Management for Office 365 and Intune . Therefore, it is important to make sure to only do the upload/renew one time, from one location , and not repeat the process more than once. If somehow the process was done multiple times and/or from different places, then the impact is that the APNs certificate that was loaded most recently will be the one that is in effect for the tenant, and any iOS devices previously enrolled (using a different APNs certificate) will no longer be managed. Using Conditional Access to manage access to Exchange Online and SharePoint Online One of the most commonly utilized feature for both Mobile Device Management for Office 365 and Intune is the Conditional Access feature which controls an organizations Exchange Online and SharePoint Online assets by granting access only to those devices that meet certain conditions (for example, encryption, password complexity, etc.) as defined by the organization. This feature is implemented somewhat differently between Mobile Device Management for Office 365 and Intune, and as a result, when used concurrently on the same tenant via the two coexisting services, one must clearly understand the differences and take the care and caution to ensure that this is configured and implemented correctly on a coexistence tenant. Key differences in the Conditional Access implementation between Intune and Mobile Device Management for Office 365: Conditional Access in Intune: Defined and targeted explicitly in the Azure portal Defined and targeted separately for Exchange Online and SharePoint Online Conditional Access in Mobile Device Management for Office 365: Defined and targeted implicitly as a part of the workflow to create and deploy a policy Defined and targeted as a whole for both Exchange Online and SharePoint Online together Key thing to keep in mind when setting/deploying Conditional Access: When setting/deploying policies in Mobile Device Management for Office 365 and/or when setting/deploying Conditional Access policies, be sure that the policies are targeted to mutually exclusive Security Groups in each case. Specifically Mobile Device Management for Office 365 policies should be targeted only to security groups (Group 1, Group 2, etc.) that ONLY include users who are managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365 Azure Conditional Access policies should be targeted only to security groups (Group A, Group B, etc.) that ONLY includes users who are managed by Intune There are no overlaps between the groups that are managed by Mobile Device Management for Office 365 and Intune

You may encounter a customer with a tenant that is already configured to integrate with Configuration Manager (i.e. a hybrid setup) but he wants to switch the the tenant so that it integrates with a different CM environment. Per the product team, below are the best practices for doing this.

Within the Device Compliance section of the Intune portal, all devices are listed as compliant, however when selecting a particular device and looking at the detailed compliance report, it shows the device as not compliant.
rootCause: This can occur if the device is compliant with Device Compliance policy but not compliant with Device Configuration policy. For example, the device may meet all the requirements for any assigned device compliance policies, but is not compliant with a device configuration profile such as a device restriction profile requiring encryption. This is by design.
solution: The labels in the portal are confusing but the reason it shows compliant one place and not the other is because under device compliance it�s actually reporting the compliance state in relation to conditional access (CA), meaning would the device be considered compliant when evaluating CA rules. In the detailed device report, that also includes device configuration profiles, which in this case was a device restriction profile that requires encryption. The device wasn�t encrypted, so it wasn�t compliant with the device restriction policy but it was compliant with the device compliance policies therefore CA should work. In other words, technically the portal is reporting accurate data, it just doesn�t seem that way because the labels and descriptions in the portal UI can be misinterpreted.

symptom: App-based conditional access policies configured to use MFA (or a password in case the app doesn't support MFA) are properly enforced with the exception of the native Mac email client, which is allowed access to email regardless of compliance.
rootCause: Currently this is by design as the native Mac email client is not supported with Intune conditional access rules.
solution: There are other ways to protect access to company email, such as requiring only the Outlook client, by implementing ADFS claims rules, etc, however each would need to be evaluated based on any other requirements and goals that the organization may have.

symptom: If a user is targeted with a Windows 10 compliance policy with any combination of three Windows 10 device health compliance settings, such as "Bitlocker is enabled", "Code Integrity is enabled", and "Secure Boot is enabled", it will cause Windows 10 1709 devices to be marked as non-compliant despite being compliant with these settings.
rootCause: The problem is caused by a product issue with the initial release of Windows 10 Fall Creators Update (RS3).
solution: The fix was included in the Windows 10 1709 December update so installing the latest Cumulative Update for Windows 10 1709 should address the issue : December 12, 2017�KB4054517 (OS Build 16299.125 - This is the minimum OS build required. Any of the newer builds should also contain the fix)https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/help/4054517

symptom: Consider the following scenario:A single user in a company is unable to login to any Office365 service when outside his company wi-fi network. Other destinations may not work as well.This user experiences the same problem even after buying a new device.The user does not experience the problem on a second device.Office mobile apps fail in the following manner:OutlookCustomer sees Outlook notifications for new emails, but when tapping the notification or going to the Inbox in Outlook, there is no new email.Intune Company Portal appWhen launching the Company Portal app when connected to a remote network, you see the following error message:ErrorCould not sign in. You will need to sign in again. If you see this message again, please contact your IT admin.OKOneDriveWhen attempting to access OneDrive when connected to a remote network, you see the following:Sign-in requiredThere's a problem with the sign-in info for "userName@contoso.com"). Please try signing in again.In all cases, the user is unable to sign in. Customer worked with his helpdesk and they tried various troubleshooting tasks in an effort to fix the problem. They even gave Brian a new Galaxy S8 device but the problems remained. - Symptoms may seem random - sometimes it works and sometimes it does not.- Symptoms are specific to a device. For example, an Android device may experience the problem but an iOS device does not..- Symptoms are totally confined to this one user - no one else at the entire company is affected.
rootCause: This can occur if the SIM card in the device is defective. In this case, when the user got a new device he kept his old SIM card which is why it appeared to follow his account. When he got a second device, the problem did not occur with his account.
solution: To resolve this problem, replace the SIM card in the device.

symptom: When trying to update an Android line of business app to a newer version via the Azure Intune portal, the following error is generated:An error occurred whilst saving. Request ID: 01a7bbff-3519-41c4-9dba-e2ca2d30ed00
rootCause: This can occur if the name of the updated package does not match the name of the package being updated. The update package name must match exactly, including case. It is case sensitive at least on Android - other platforms have not been tested.
solution: To resolve this problem, make sure the update package name matches the name of the package being updated exactly, including case.

After assigning an app to a group of Windows 10 clients as a Required app, the app will initially be installed, however if the end user manually removes the app they are never prompted to reinstall the app again.
rootCause: This is by design for Windows. Currently only iOS will automatically attempt to reinstall a manually deleted app that was deployed by Intune as Required.
solution: As a work around, create a second group that contains the same Windows 10 users, then create a new deployment for the same app and make the assignment type Available. After doing this you should have two assignments for the same app - one Required and one Available. Essentially it'll be something like this:MyApp - WindowsGroup1 - RequiredMyApp - WindowsGroup2 - AvailableConfigured like this, all users in WindowsGroup1 will have the app automatically installed, and if they ever manually remove the app then the second assignment as an Available app will populate that app in the web portal, giving the user a way to reinstall the app.Also, the Intune Administrator can re-trigger the required deployment by removing the group from the app assignment set to Required, then adding that group back. After doing this, any users who already have the app are unaffected, however any users who have removed the app will have it reinstalled.

symptom: When setting the Intune Exchange service to server connector you receive the following error message  error message An error occurred request ID : 6f140b49-b2bd-4e29-b335-5b8c053c354d
rootCause: This issue can be caused by  account used to setup the connector does not have either Intune license or global administrator account.
solution: To resolve the issue assign the account used to setup the Intune connector an Intune license or assign it global admin role.

symptom: In the Azure Intune portal under Devices -> All Devices, some devices show an email address and UPN for the owner while others do not. You may also notice that for the devices that do not show these properties, the devices has not checked in for many days.
rootCause: This can occur if the users with missing information originally had a valid Intune license, but at some time after enrollment the license was removed or it expired.
solution: To resolve this problem, go to the Office 365 portal -> Users and assign the affected user an Intune license (or an E3, G3, E5, license, etc.). Once this is done, the devices should populate the missing information after the next sync.

When attempting to enroll a Windows Phone in Microsoft Intune, the enrollment process fails with the following error:This account is not allowed on this phone. Make sure the information you provided is correct, and then try again or request support from your company.
rootCause: This can occur if the user enrolling the phone does not have a valid Intune license.
solution: To resolve this problem, assign the user a valid Intune license then attempt to enroll the device again.

symptom: Attempting to enroll a Windows 10 PC fails with the following error:Something went wrong.  The device is already enrolled.  You can contact your system administrator with the error code 8018000a.
rootCause: This can occur if a different user on the Windows device has already enrolled the PC in MDM or joined Azure AD. To verify if this is your problem, go to Settings -> Accounts -> Work Access and look for a message similar to the following:Another user on the system is already connected to a work or school. Please remove that work or school connection and try again.
solution: To resolve this problem, complete the following:1. Log out of Windows, then log back in using the other account. 2. Go to Settings -> Accounts -> Work Access and remove the computer form the work or school account.3. Log out of Windows, then log back in using the first account.4. Attempt to enroll the device or join Azure AD.At this point enrollment should now succeed.

symptom: When attempting to enroll a Windows 10 PC, the enrollment process fails with the following:This user is not allowed to enroll. You can try again or contact your system administrator with the error code 801c0003.
rootCause: This can occur if the setting in Azure Active Directory for devices to join Azure AD was set to NONE. this will block all new users from joining their devices to Azure AD, thus breaking MDM enrollment if auto-MDM enrollment is enabled.
solution: To resolve this problem, complete the following:1. Open a browser and navigate to https://portal.azure.com.2. Login with administrator credentials.3. Select Azure Active Directory -> Users and Groups -> Device Settings.6. Set the option "Users may register their devices with Azure AD" to "ALL" Once this is done, try the enrollment or Azure AD join again. At this point the process should complete successfully.

symptom: Consider the following scenario: You are attempting to create a provisioning package using Windows Configuration Designer (WCD). When going to the Account Management step, selecting "Enroll in Azure AD" and then clicking on the "Get Bulk Token" button, the following error is received Bulk token retrieval failed. The operation returned an empty response. Please try again.
rootCause: This can occur if there are no more licenses available for Azure AD Premium and Microsoft Intune.
solution: To resolve this problem, either purchase additional licenses for the tenant or remove any unneeded licenses from unused package users. Once licenses become available, package creation should succeed.

symptom: When attempting to enroll a Windows 10 computer, the following error is displayed:This user is not authorized to enroll. You can try to do this again or contact your system administrator with the error code (0x801c0003).
rootCause: This can occur if the user has already enrolled the maximum number of allowed devices in Intune.
solution: To resolve this problem, you either need to remove devices already enrolled by the user, increase the number of devices users are allowed to enroll, or configure a Device Enrollment Manager (DEM) account.To remove unused devices already enrolledRemove any stale or unneeded devices listed for the user in the Company Portal and the Azure Intune all Users portal.To increase the number of devices users are allowed to enroll1. Go to the Azure Intune portal.2. Navigate to Device Enrollment -> Enrollment Restrictions and select Device Limit Restrictions.3. Increase the value for Device Limit.Note that this will increase the enrollment limit for all users, not just the affected user.To configure a DEM accountTo add and use a DEM account, see the article Enroll devices using device enrollment manager for details.

symptom: When attempting to enroll a Windows 10 Home computer, the following error is displayed:This user is not authorized to enroll. You can try to do this again or contact your system administrator with the error code (0x801c0003).
rootCause: This is expected for Windows 10 Home Edition. Enrolling in Intune or joining Azure AD is only support on Windows 10 Professional and higher versions.
solution: To resolve this problem, upgrade the device to Windows 10 Pro or better.

symptom: When attempting to enroll a Windows 10 computer in Intune, or join a Windows 10 computer to Azure Active Directory, the following error is displayed:Looks like the MDM Terms of Use endpoint is not correctly configured
rootCause: This can occur if the Intune tenant is configured for co-existence with MDM for Office 365, and the user account being used to enroll the device does not have an Intune license or a license for Mobile Device Management for Office 365.
solution: To resolve this problem, use the Office365 admin portal to assign the user either an Intune license or a Mobile Device Management for Office 365 license, as appropriate.

symptom:
This article
describes a procedure to fix issues when MSCEP-RA expires. When you try
to access the NDES URL on a browser and receive error: "500 - Internal Server error" The
following errors are found in the Event Viewer logs (application):-The Network

solution:
1.- Checking
Permissions on Managed Key On the NDES
server, on the certificate that we created, please check the Private key
permissions:          2.- Check
status of the service account:    3.- How
to Request new MSCEP-RA certificates  NOTE: It is really important to set the correct expectations to the customer at this point. As we do not have the Intune connector installed at this moment, this is NOT an Intune issue, and is something fails, the Windows Directory Services team should be involved. MSCEP-RA certificates get created when you install the NDES role on the server. This procedure is a workaround to avoid having to reinstall the role.  1.- Check Template permissions  Logged on
User and NDES computer must have read and

symptom: When contacting Microsoft Support for help with Microsoft Intune, Enterprise Mobility Suite (EMS), Mobile Device Management (MDM) or Mobile Application Management, there are a few things that you can do first that may help you get a resolution to your problem faster.

You notice that the Intune Company Portal app for Android no longer receives automatic updates from the managed Google Play store. As a result, the Company Portal app becomes out-of-date and fails to receive important bug fixes or feature updates.
rootCause: This can occur if the Company Portal app has not been manually approved in the managed Google Play store. This needs to be done only one time and the steps are below.
solution: To manually approve the Company Portal app, complete the following steps:1. Browse to the Intune Company Portal in the managed Play Store by following this URL: https://play.google.com/work/apps/details?id=com.microsoft.windowsintune.companyportal2. Sign in to the managed Google Play store using the same Google account you used to configure your Android for Work binding. If you forget what account you used, you can view it in the Intune admin page on the Device enrollment -> Android for Work enrollment blade under Google Account.3. In the Intune Company Portal listing in the managed Google Play store, click Approve.4. Review the permissions dialog and click Approve. These permissions are necessary for the Company Portal to manage the work profile on devices managed by your organization:5. It is recommended that you select Keep approved when app requests new permissions so that the app will stay approved in the event that permissions change. You can optionally sign up for email notifications of permissions changes on the Notifications tab. Click Save.6. Verify that the Approved label appears as shown below.7. You app is now approved and should automatically receive updates in the future.

solution: In order to remote wipe these devices, the device ownership category must be changed from "Personal" to "Corporate".

Something went
wrong. This user is not authorized to enroll. You can try to do this again or
contact your system administrator with the error code 801c0003.
rootCause:
This
can occur if the user is not allowed to join devices to Azure AD.
solution:
To resolve this
problem, allow Azure AD Join for All or Selected users: 

 

Login to https://portal.azure.com with your
administrator credentials. 

Select Azure Active Directory on the left.

Go to Devices and then select Device Settings. 

Set Users may join devices to Azure AD to All or Selected.

If using Selected, click on Selected below. Then click Add Members and provide the users who should be allowed to join devices to Azure AD.

Content Idea Auto Publish can now extract the content from a Word document and create a Portico article for you. This is very convenient and quick, just author in Word and attach the doc to the CI bug. This also enables richer formatting options than available in the TFS fields.
  
  
     
  
  
    1.
    Open a Content Idea TFS work item to create a new internal KB article.
  
  
    2.
    Click the link �Create your article using Word� to download a template, and use it to create your article.
  
  
     
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
     
  
  
    Note:
     The downloadable template can populate the various content fields in the CI bug. You can also use any Word doc as a source, but all the content will be extracted into the �Symptom/Summary� field.
  
  
     
  
  
    3.
    Instead of pasting the content into the content fields, just attach the word doc to the CI bug. 
  
  
    4.
    Save.
  
  
    5.

After configuring hybrid Azure AD per https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/device-management-hybrid-azuread-joined-devices-setup, existing Windows 10 computers are not automatically enrolled in Intune. Because these computers are not MDM enrolled, they are noncompliant, meaning they fail any Conditional Access checks.
rootCause: This can occur for two reasons:1. The GPO for auto enrollment per https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/client-management/mdm/enroll-a-windows-10-device-automatically-using-group-policy is not configured. 2. The Windows 10 client PCs are running a build of Windows prior to 1709 (e.g. build 1703), meaning auto enrollment won't work even if the GPO is setup.
solution: To work around this problem, either upgrade the client PCs to Windows 10 build 1709 and implement the auto-enrollment GPO per the article above, or instruct end users to manually enroll in MDM. This would work similar to manually enrolling a personal iOS or Android device in a BYOD scenario.

symptom: When attempting to update the version of a deployed Line-of-Business app using the Azure Intune portal, the OK button never lights up, meaning you are not allowed to upload the newer version of the IPA or APK file.
rootCause: Varies
solution: To troubleshoot this issue, attempt to update the app using the classic portal (Silverlight portal) and see if the app can be updated there. If it can, there's a problem specific to the Azure Intune portal and will require an IcM. If it still fails, chances are good that you will get a more informative error that will help you troubleshoot the problem. For example, you may get the following error in the classic portal:The software you specified could not be updated. This is because the software you provided is an earlier version than the current software. to use this software, create and deploy it as a new application.

symptom: When saving Outlook email attachments to a USB drive the user is prompted to 'save as work protected' However Windows Information Protection policy does not encrypt the file. The attachments can still be opened on another device.EDP-Audit-TCB log shows Enterprise contoso.onmicrosoft.com tag has been removed (Protection removed) from the file

When attempting to launch Outlook on iOS devices, the following message is displayed:Warning You need to launch the company portal to guarantee future app access. Press OK to exit the app, and then open the Company Portal.Not Now | OKClicking 'Not Now' takes the use into the Outlook app but the message soon reappears. Clicking OK closes the app and the error message appears again the next time Outlook is launched.
rootCause: This can occur if the certificate is nearing expiration. Typically, these certs need to be renewed every year. To avoid any loss of functionality, the iOS Company Portal (CP) app will begin attempting to renew certificates once the expiration date is 90 days away. However, to complete this renewal process the iOS CP app needs to be opened. If the iOS CP is not opened and the certificate expiration date becomes 45 days or less, MAM enabled apps will warn the user to open the CP app to avoid future app launch failure. This can also happen if the User Principal Name (UPN) has been changed. Search the Company Portal logs for the UPN. If the current UPN is not found in the Company Portal logs, try searching the CP logs for the domain (i.e., contoso.com) and confirm if a different UPN is located. If you locate a different UPN and do not find the current UPN in the CP logs, it is likely that the device has not been re-enrolled using the current UPN.
solution: Normally, once the CP app is opened, the certificate will renew and the user will then be able to use the app normally. In other cases, such as that in which a UPN change occurred, unenrolling and re-enrolling the device is necessary to make the errors go away. In certain rare cases, the device had to be wiped (retired, or remove company data) and then re-enrolled to resolve the issue.

rootCause: **Collecting log filesRequesting the customer to send the Windows Update log file you can get details related to the problem, find the error message and avoid many troubleshooting steps that will not solve the issue.You can find WindowsUpdate.log file at %windir% for older versions of windows. NOTE: For Windows 10 machines, execute the PowerShell command �Get-WindowsUpdateLog� to convert the EVT files into readable logs.  Move the WindowsUpdate.log from the user�s Desktop into the %windir% folder.IMPORTANT: Use the MSDT package �Performance Maintenance\Windows Update � Collect Data� to upload the log files directly into the SR.
solution: Delete

symptom: After configuring Exchange Online, Windows 10 users receive errors similar to the following when using the Office 365 apps such as Outlook, Excel, Word, PowerPoint, etc.Can't use work content hereYour organization doesn't allow you to use work content here.orThe snip could not be sent. Check that an email program is installed and then try again.
rootCause: This can occur if the default Windows device policies in the O365 portal are applied to All Users:Encrypt work files = OnRestrict copying of company data = OnEnable recovery of protected data = OnProtect additional company cloud locations = OnFiles used by these apps are protected = <all O365 apps>
solution: To resolve this problem, assuming the customer does not want this functionality, simply remove those policies.Note that in my case, the customer didn't want to delete the policies so instead he assigned them to an empty group, however the policies remained. My assumption is that this is because since there are no actual UPNs to change the assignment to, whatever rule kicks off to change the assignment effectively does nothing. To work around this, a new group was created that contained a single, unused UPN, then the assignments for these policies were configured only for the new group, effectively removing All Users. Once that was done, the policies were no longer applied and the messages above were no longer generated.

symptom: When attempting to update an Android or iOS app, the following error message is displayed:The software you specified could not be updated because it has different deployment requirements than the current software. If you want to deploy this software, you must create and deploy it as a new application.
rootCause: This can occur if the initial APK or IPA file was uploaded without the app wrapper, and you are trying to upload a newer version of the file that is using the app wrapper. Or vice versa.
solution: To resolve this problem:If the initial upload was done with the wrapper, the update must include the wrapper as well. If the initial upload was done without the wrapper, the update must be done without the wrapper as well.

symptom: When attempting to enroll a Windows 10 device into hybrid mobile device management with SCCM and  Intune, the following error is displayed"There was a problem. Your organization does not support this version of Windows.  (0x80180014)".
rootCause: This error will occur if Windows enrollment has been disabled for the organization.
solution: To resolve this problem, enable Windows enrollment as detailed below:    In your SCCM console, go to Administration, then select Cloud Services. Right-click Microsoft Intune Subscription and select Configure Platforms/ Windows. Check Enable Windows enrollment. Hit Apply, then OK. Note: If you are receiving the error in an Intune Standalone environment, follow the solution from this article instead.

symptom: When attempting to install the Intune PC client software, the setup process fails with the following error:The software cannot be installed, 0x80cf4017.
rootCause: This can occur if the PC client package being used is out of date.
solution: To resolve this problem, download a new copy of the Intune PC client agent installer and try the setup process again.

symptom: When attempting to enroll a Windows computer using the Intune PC client software, the enrollment process fails with the following error:The account certificate is not valid and may be expired, 0x80cf4017
rootCause: This can occur if the PC client package being used is out of date.
solution: To resolve this problem, download a new copy of the Intune PC client agent installer and try the setup process again.

symptom: When attempting to enroll a Windows 10 computer in Intune, or join a Windows 10 computer to Azure Active Directory, the following error is displayed:Looks like the MDM Terms of Use endpoint is not correctly configured
rootCause: This can occur if the MDM terms and conditions in Azure AD is blank or does not contain the correct URL
solution: To resolve this problem, ensure that the MDM terms of use URL in Azure AD has the correct URL:Login to https://portal.azure.com with your administrator credentials. Select Azure Active Directory on the left.Go to Mobility (MDM and MAM). Select Microsoft Intune. Click on the option Restore default MDM URLs.Verify that MDM terms of use URL is set to: https://portal.manage.microsoft.com/TermsofUse.aspxClick Save.

symptom: When bulk enrolling Windows devices, you receive the following error message during the Enrolls the device in management step:A setup failure has occurred
rootCause: This issue can occur if the Azure AD user account in the account package (Package_GUID) for the respective provisioning package isn't allowed to join devices to Azure AD. These Azure AD accounts are automatically created when you set up a provisioning package with Windows Configuration Designer (WCD) or the Set up School PCs app. These accounts are then used to join the devices to Azure AD.
solution: To resolve this problem, allow Azure AD Join for All or include the Azure AD package_xxxxxx account(s) to Selected users: Login to https://portal.azure.com with your administrator credentials. Select Azure Active Directory on the left.Go to Devices and then select Device Settings. Set Users may join devices to Azure AD to All or Selected. Note that if you choose Selected, click Selected and then click Add Members to add all users who are allowed to join their devices to Azure AD. Make sure that all Azure AD accounts for the provisioning package are added.

symptom: Occasionally you might get a customer asking you about �device licenses� within Intune. Eg; this article mentions it� https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/licenses-assign Each device that you assign a device software license to may access and use the online services and related software (including System Center software) for use by any number of users. There are a few important things to note: 1) You cannot manually �assign� a license to a device. This terminology is misleading and some customers will misinterpret this due to it being unclear. 2) This means the device ITSELF can access the service � however, this is not enough. +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++ The next line (from the above article) says� If a device is used by more than one user, each requires a device software license, or all users require a user software license. This implies a few important notes. 1) If it is a userless device (e.g.; a Kiosk DEP device without User Affinity that customers � who obviously can�t be licensed � use primarily). a. There is no technical restriction to enrolling the device. It will be able to enroll fine (via DEP, or DEM devices via the Company Portal). b. The DEM account must be licensed (to provision the account into Intune) but this does not license the devices. c. There must be an Intune license owned by the customer for this userless device. 2) If it is a multi-user device (eg; a DEM-enrolled device that might be shared among various people on a team) a. There must be a license for the device (a single Intune license) b. OR c. The employees using the device need to be licensed. Here are some of the articles that mention Device Licenses (and �Assigning� them): Enroll devices using device enrollment manager Automatically enroll iOS devices with Apple's Device Enrollment Program In a nutshell � the main things to note, and take away from this are� 1) You cannot �assign� device licenses. 2) If you have the same number of devices as you do users (1,000 users, 1,000 devices, and 1,000 EM+S licenses) � you are good to go. 3) If you have a higher number of devices than users, or a large amount of �userless� devices, you should account for these. 4) The customer should purchase as many device licenses as they have userless devices, including DEM-managed devices. See �More Information� for a few example scenarios.

symptom: Some admins may want to restrict Exchange Active Sync access to the Outlook app only in order to enforce MAM policies and managed email profiles for enabling selective wipes of company data. Here is how to set up Intune together with Exchange Online/Exchange On Prem to block native EAS apps. (Requires EMS or Azure Premium+Intune)(Exchange On Premise requires Hybrid Modern Auth)Use Azure Conditional Access to Only Allow approved clients appsNavigate to https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_AAD_IAM/ConditionalAccessBlade/PoliciesCreate a new policyUnder Cloud Apps, select Office 365 Exchange OnlineUnder Conditions / Client Apps pick only 'Exchange Active Sync'Under Access Control / Grant click Grant access and check 'Required approved client app" If you want to require MDM enrollment against the Outlook app then create a 2nd policyNote: We recommend only targeting Exchange and/or SharePoint. If you wish to use CA for other O365 resources then create a separate policy for those that odes not require an approved client appUnder Conditions / Client Apps pick "Select client apps" and put a check in "Mobile apps" (Mobile apps would apply to Outlook app) Under Access Control / Grant click Grant access and check "Require device to be compliant and 'Required approved client app" Select "Require all the selected controls"You should create a separate CA policy for Windows devices that you require to be domain joinedThis setup will automatically block the Native Email clients as they are incapable of becoming compliant and force the device that runs the Outlook app to be MDM enrolled

rootCause: Unsupported
solution: Available Install is not supported for mobile applications to a device group.This includes: Windows App PackageApp package for mobile devicesWindows InstallerManaged iOS app from the App Store

symptom: After configuring a Device Restriction policy that sets "Manual Unenrollment" to "Block" per the documentation here, the users who are assigned the policy are still able to manually unenroll their Windows 10 computers.
rootCause: This can occur if the Windows computer is Azure AD joined and MDM auto-enrolled in Intune. It can also occur if the version of Windows 10 being targeted by the policy is Windows 10 Home.
solution: If you wish you use this policy setting, you will need to either disable auto-enrollment and/or make sure the users are using Windows 10 Pro or enterprise.

symptom: Attempting to enroll an iOS device using the Company Portal app fails with the following error: Company Portal Temporarily Unavailable
rootCause: This can occur if the Azure Active Directory Enterprise Applications Microsoft Intune or Microsoft Intune Enrollment has the setting Enabled for users to sign-in? set to a value of No.
solution: To resolve this problem complete the following: 1. Open a browser and navigate to the Azure Active Directory blade at https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_AAD_IAM/ActiveDirectoryMenuBlade/Overview. 2. Go to Enterprise Applications and click on Microsoft Intune and/or Microsoft Intune Enrollment, then click Properties. Make sure that Enabled for user sign-in? is set to a value of Yes and save your change, then try enrolling the device again.

symptom: When logging on as another user to a Surface Hub you receive an error message similar to this Devices or client applications that meet <Tenant Name>  management compliance policy If this is personal device you can let <Tenant Name> manage your device by going to Settings > Accounts > Access Work or school and clicking in "Connect".  When you're dome come back and try again.  Please contact your administrator The following information might be useful to your administrator: Access Rules set by <Tenant Name> require a compliant device.  App name:  SurfaceHub AAD LoginApp id:  <GUID> IP address:  1.1.1.1 Device identifier: not available Device platform: Windows 10Device State: Unregistered Singed in as:  User@domain.com Correlation ID: <GUID> Timestamp: <Date and time Stamp>
rootCause: You receiving this error because there is conditional access policy that is targeting the Surface Hub.   Surface Hubs do not support Azure Conditional Access Policies.
solution: To resolve this issue create a new security group.  Move all the Surface Hub machines into the new security group.   Then add the new security group to the excluded user setting on the conditional access policy.

rootCause: In SA scenario: the crl is not reachable from the ndes serverIn hybrid scenario: the crl is not reachable from the CRP server  403.13 stands for Client Certificate Revoked, but our client certificate is valid so we need to make sure we have the crl reachable.
solution:
Enable
IIS to download the CRL. To do this, follow these steps:


 Delete any duplicate client certificates (that
     is, client certificates that are issued from the same Certificate
     Authority) from the client browser.
 Start with the client certificate and proceed up the
     certification path. Paste each certificate's CDP HTTP reference in the
     browser on the server. If the file fails to download, there is a problem
     with the CDP.NOTE: Double-click each certificate in the certification path
     to view its properties.
 Use the PING, Tracert.exe, or Wfetch.exe utilities to
     identify any name resolution or network latency issues that arise when you
     contact the problem CDP.
 Find the IP address of the problem CDP and add an entry
     to the HOSTS file on the IIS computer. This should enable IIS to download
     the CRL and resolve the error.
 Repeat these steps for each certificate in the client
     certificate's certification path

symptom:
Configuring Symantec PKI manager for Intune PKCS deployment.
  
  
    Intune has the capability to deploy PKCS certificates from Symantec PKI  manager web service. The process for configuring this is largely covered in the following document:
  
    
      https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/certificates-symantec-configure
    
  
  
    However, what is not documented but is alluded to, is how to configure the certificate template from the Symantec PKI Manager itself.
  
  
    At a very high level the process is as follows:
  
  
    1.
    Install/Configure the Intune certificate connector on an on-premise server.
  
  
    2.
    Request the Symantec RA certificate. At point 4 in the documented process the request for the RA cert is at the bottom of the list of actions in the settings (see below):
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
    And it looks like this (you paste in the CSR created by the cert request in step 2 of the Install Symantec RA certificate).
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
    3.
    Install the RA cert on the machine where the connector is installed in the personal certificate store in the computer context ensuring that the certificate has the private key:
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
    You can see both the root and client certs in the screenshot above. It is critical that the certificate has the private key otherwise this connection to Symantec will fail. This is what is documented in steps 5-7 in the install the Symantec Registration Authorization Certificate section.
  
  
    4.
    Make sure you copy the RA cert thumbprint (i.e. the Registration Authority cert) and paste this in the %ProgramFiles%\Microsoft Intune\NDESConnectorSvc\NDESConnector.exe.config file, save it, and then restart the Intune connector service.
  
  
     
  
  
    5.
    Create a trusted certificate profile on Symantecs site
  
  
     
  
  
    To do this you need to log on to the Symantec PKI manager and create a certificate profile.
  
  
     
  
  
    
    Click on Add certificate profiles at the top right (highlighted)
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
     
  
  
    
    Select either test or production mode (either will work)
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
    
    Select the client authentication option
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
     
  
  
    
    Enter a name
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
    
    Click on Enrollment method and select PKI web services
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
     
  
  
    
    Click on advanced options and delete Common Name (CN) using the x 
  
  
    
    Add field > select Common Name (CN), Webservice Request, required = Yes 
  
    All the other advanced settings can be left with the default values but check you are matching the validity period in the template with the Intune policy and if the target is iOS you may want to deselect non-repudiation in the Key Usage (KU)
  
  
     
  
  
    
    Save the certificate template and make a note of the OID. When you click on the certificate template it is located on the right as follows:
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
      
  
    6.
    Configure a PKCS certificate profile in Intune and deploy this to a group
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
    It is important to keep the Certification authority and Certificate authority name exactly as above.

At the beginning of February, Microsoft publicly released the information that most of the customers were looking for: ability to pre-configure the email settings for the Outlook application.The documentation about how to configure these settings were presented in the following link. The important steps in configuring this are:1. Deploy and assign the Outlook app - iOS/AFW2. Create the App Configuration policy for iOS/AFW     a. Link the app with the Outlook App that was created at step 1;     b. Configure the settings based on the documentation presented above;Even though the configuration is made by the book, Outlook application is not pre-configured. No email account is found when you start the application.The expected behavior would have been for the user to find his email address and Exchange server pre-configured for him.
rootCause: This issue was encountered because the Keys mentioned in the documentation are not correct(or they were not enabled yet in the Outlook app).The wrong(not working at the moment) Keys are:com.microsoft.outlook.EmailProfile.EmailAccountNamecom.microsoft.outlook.EmailProfile.EmailAddresscom.microsoft.outlook.EmailProfile.EmailUPNcom.microsoft.outlook.EmailProfile.ServerAuthenticationcom.microsoft.outlook.EmailProfile.ServerHostName
solution:
I managed to pre-configure the Outlook email profile settings by using the Keys from the below table. These Keys are accepted by the Outlook apps and helps the users to have their settings pre-configured. 
 
  
  Key
  
  
  Value
  
 
 
  
   
   
  com.outlook.emailAddress
  
  
  This value specifies the email address to be used for sending
  and receiving mail.
  Accepted values: String
  Default if not specified: <blank>
  Example:
  user@companyname.com
  Intune Token: {{mail}}
  
 
 
  
   
   
  com.outlook.accountName
  
  
  This value specifies the display name email account as it will
  appear to users on their devices .
  Accepted values: String
  Default if not specified: <blank>
  Example:
  user@companyname.com
  Intune Token: {{username}}
  
 
 
  
  com.outlook.emailServer
  
  
  This value specifies the host name of your Exchange server.
  Accepted values: String
  Default if not specified: <blank>
  
 
 
  
   
  com.outlook.domain
  
  
  This
  value specifies the Domain for the email profile.
  Accepted values: String
  Default if not specified: <blank>
  
 
 
  
   
   
  com.outlook.username
  
  
  This value specifies the User Principal Name for the email
  profile that will be used to authenticate the account.
  Accepted values: String
  Default if not specified: <blank>
  Example:
  userupn@companyname.com
  Intune Token: {{partialupn}}
  
 
 
  
   
  com.outlook.serverAuthentication
  
  
  This value specifies the authentication method for the user.
  Accepted values: 'Username and Password'; 'Certificates'
  Default if not specified: 'Username and Password'
  Example:
  'Username and Password'

When assigning a Device Compliance policy to a group of Windows computers, the policy is applied as expected but duplicate records are created for each device in the policy Device Status list.
rootCause: This can occur if the group assigned the device compliance policy is a dynamic device group.
solution: To work around this issue, create a manually assigned device group or a user group and assign the policy to that group instead.Another alternative is described at https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/windows-enrollIf you do not have Auto-MDM enrollment enabled, but you have Windows 10 devices that have been joined to Azure AD, two records will be visible in the Intune console after enrollment. You can stop this by making sure that users with Azure AD joined devices go to Accounts > Access work or school and Connect using the same account.

rootCause:
Azure
Active Directory has the Intune service principals disabled. Hence, when the tenant admin logs in, they are
unable to get a user token for Intune from AAD because AAD thinks that the
tenant does not have Intune.
solution:
To resolve this issue, follow the steps below:1) Ensure that the UPN is indeed a GA (Global Admin) and is Intune licensed2) Ensure that MDM authority is not Office365 or SCCM3) Capture the E12 error that is shown after an unsuccessfully login, for example:https://sts.manage.microsoft.com/Error?E=12&supportid=63E5890F-F258-4044-8652-1AA987372743Please Note: You will need to wait several minutes to proceed to the next steps so that Kusto can shown respective data 4) Execute the following Kusto query and look for the following exception to get the Service Principle ApplicationId:

IntuneEvent
| where ActivityId  == "2d0ee520-94a5-4c0a-9339-b027840ea3ca"
(This is E12 support ID error in lowercase that the customer
gets when after an unsuccessful login)
| where * contains "exception" Exception:�Microsoft.IdentityModel.Clients.ActiveDirectory.AdalServiceException:
AADSTS70001: Application '9CB77803-D937-493E-9A3B-4B49DE3F5A74' is disabled.�  5) Login to portal.azure.com -> Azure Active Directory -> Enterprise Applications and search using the ApplicationId found above: 6) Select the application and click properties and ensure that the option "Enabled for users to sign-in?" is set to "Yes"7) Have the Intune license GA attempt to login again to manage.microsoft.com and confirm that the login is now successfulPlease note: You may have multiple Intune service principals that are disabled for the tenant so you will need to verify each of them. Here is the full list of Intune service principals and their ApplicationId:Microsoft Intune -> 0000000a-0000-0000-c000-000000000000Microsoft Intune Enrollment -> d4ebce55-015a-49b5-a083-c84d1797ae8cMicrosoft Intune Service Discovery -> 9cb77803-d937-493e-9a3b-4b49de3f5a74Microsoft Mobile Application Management -> 0a5f63c0-b750-4f38-a71c-4fc0d58b89e2

    Based on Configuration Manager Current Branch Licensing documentation, EMS licenses are eligible to use for installing SCCM Current Branch:
  
  
    
      
      
      https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sccm/core/understand/product-and-licensing-faq
      
    
  
  
    Only customers with active Software Assurance on System Center Configuration Manager Licenses, or with equivalent subscription rights, may install and use the Current Branch of System Center Configuration Manager.

The Microsoft Volume Licensing Product Terms refers to these programs as {Namepii} License Equivalent Licenses.
